title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Silent Eloquence of the Digital Dialect
content.subtitle: How internet-mediated communication is enriching, rather than eroding, the human language.
content.text: The perennial lament among traditionalists is that the rise of digital communication—texting, instant messaging, and social media—heralds the death of formal prose. They point to the ubiquity of abbreviations, the disregard for standard punctuation, and the 'degeneration' of grammar as evidence of a looming intellectual decline. [31] However, this perspective overlooks the fact that language has always been a fluid entity, constantly recalibrating itself to meet the needs of its speakers and the constraints of its medium. [/31] Rather than a decay, what we are witnessing is the birth of a sophisticated 'fingered speech'—a hybrid that sits comfortably between the formality of writing and the spontaneity of conversation. || In speech, we rely heavily on non-verbal cues: tone of voice, facial expressions, and physical gestures. These elements provide the emotional context that raw text inherently lacks. To compensate for this 'signal deficit,' digital users have developed an intricate system of paralanguage. [32] The use of emojis, capital letters for emphasis, and even the strategic omission of a final period are not signs of laziness, but are essential tools for conveying nuance and social intent. [/32] A message that ends with a full stop can now be perceived as cold or even aggressive, whereas its absence signals a more relaxed, ongoing dialogue. || [33] Furthermore, the speed of digital exchange has necessitated a level of linguistic economy that is often mistaken for a lack of sophistication. [/33] In reality, creating a perfectly timed, culturally resonant 'meme' or a concise acronym requires a deep understanding of shared social context and subtext. This is not the work of the linguistically impoverished; it is the work of a highly connected generation that values efficiency and emotional resonance over the rigid structures of the past. The digital dialect is, in many ways, more inclusive, allowing for a rapid synthesis of global slang and local idioms. || [34] Linguists have also noted that the internet has created a 'leveling' effect, where the distinction between high and low culture in language is becoming increasingly blurred. [/34] Academic jargon and street slang now coexist in the same digital spaces, leading to a cross-pollination of terms that enriches the lexicon. A term coined in a niche online community can become part of the global vernacular in a matter of days. This acceleration of linguistic evolution is unprecedented in human history, making the current era one of the most vibrant periods for language development since the invention of the printing press. || Despite these benefits, some worry that the dominance of English in digital spaces will lead to a 'linguistic monoculture,' erasing the unique characteristics of smaller languages. [35] While it is true that English remains the lingua franca of the web, the reality is more nuanced, as many communities are using digital tools to revitalize endangered dialects and document oral traditions that were on the brink of extinction. [/35] The internet is not just a platform for globalization; it is also a powerful tool for local preservation. || [36] Ultimately, the fear that digital communication will destroy our ability to think deeply or write formally is likely unfounded. [/36] Throughout history, new forms of communication—from the telegraph to the telephone—have sparked similar anxieties about the 'end of civilization.' In each case, humans have simply added a new register to their linguistic repertoire. We are not losing our ability to use formal language; we are simply becoming more versatile, learning to switch between the rigid requirements of an essay and the playful, efficient shorthand of the digital age with increasing ease.

questions.question: What is the author's primary counter-argument to the 'traditionalist' view in the first paragraph?
options: A) Formal prose is no longer necessary in the modern professional world.
B) Digital language is more intellectually demanding than traditional writing.
C) Language is naturally adaptive and changes to suit new methods of communication.
D) Abbreviations and poor grammar have always been a part of human speech.
questions.explanation: The author argues that language is a 'fluid entity' that is 'constantly recalibrating itself' to meet the needs of its medium, rather than just decaying.

questions.question: The author mentions the 'strategic omission of a final period' to show that...
options: A) modern writers have forgotten the basic rules of punctuation.
B) punctuation is becoming irrelevant in all forms of writing.
C) digital users use text to replicate the emotional cues of spoken language.
D) text messages are inherently more aggressive than other forms of communication.
questions.explanation: The text explains that these are 'essential tools for conveying nuance' to compensate for the 'signal deficit' of raw text, providing emotional context.

questions.question: In the third paragraph, the author suggests that linguistic economy in digital communication...
options: A) is a sign that users are becoming less sophisticated over time.
B) requires a high degree of cultural awareness and social understanding.
C) is primarily driven by a desire to exclude older generations.
D) makes it impossible for users to express complex emotions.
questions.explanation: The author states that creating memes or acronyms requires a 'deep understanding of shared social context and subtext,' calling it the work of a 'highly connected generation.'

questions.question: What is meant by the 'leveling' effect mentioned in the fourth paragraph?
options: A) The internet has forced everyone to use a more academic style of writing.
B) Cultural differences are disappearing because of global internet access.
C) There is a growing mixture of formal and informal language in digital spaces.
D) Most people now prefer street slang over professional terminology.
questions.explanation: The text explains this as the blurring of 'high and low culture' where academic jargon and street slang 'coexist' and cross-pollinate.

questions.question: Regarding the fear of 'linguistic monoculture,' the author points out that...
options: A) English will soon be the only language used on the internet.
B) digital platforms are actually being used to save dying languages.
C) smaller languages are not worth preserving in a globalized world.
D) the web has no impact on the survival of local dialects.
questions.explanation: The text says that many communities are using digital tools to 'revitalize endangered dialects' and document traditions, acting as a tool for 'local preservation.'

questions.question: What conclusion does the author reach in the final paragraph?
options: A) Digital communication is indeed making us less capable of deep thought.
B) Historical anxieties about new communication tools were usually correct.
C) Humans are developing the ability to use different styles of language for different contexts.
D) Formal writing will eventually be replaced by digital shorthand in all areas of life.
questions.explanation: The author states we are 'adding a new register' and 'learning to switch' between formal requirements and digital shorthand with ease.
